title: Cat Eye Care: What to Do If a Foreign Object Is Floating on Your Cat's Eye
meta_desc: Find out what to do if your cat has something in its eye. Learn why this is a medium-urgency issue and how photos can help your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is a Foreign Object on a Cat's Eye?

When you notice something floating on the surface of your cat's eye, it is often a piece of debris like hair, dust, or plant material trapped in the tear film. While the eye has natural mechanisms to flush these out, objects can sometimes become stuck or cause microscopic scratches on the cornea, leading to discomfort or secondary infections.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Finding a foreign object in your cat's eye is considered a Medium Urgency situation. If your cat is acting normally and the object is clearly floating, it may not be an immediate crisis. However, if your cat is squinting, pawing at the face, or if the eye appears red or cloudy, you should seek veterinary care within 24 hours. Prompt attention helps prevent a simple irritation from turning into a painful corneal ulcer.

Why Taking a Photo Can Help

Capturing a clear, close-up photo of the eye can be an invaluable tool for your veterinary team. A high-quality image allows a professional to triage the situation remotely by determining if the object is truly floating or if it has become embedded in the ocular tissue. This helps them advise you on whether you should attempt a gentle saline flush at home or head to the clinic immediately.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops to flush my cat's eye?
faqs.answer: You should only use a sterile, plain saline wash specifically labeled for eyes. Never use medicated human drops or redness-relief drops, as these can contain ingredients that are toxic or painful for cats.

faqs.question: What if the object disappears but the eye is still red?
faqs.answer: If the object is gone but your cat continues to squint or the eye remains red, the object may have left a scratch on the cornea. This requires a veterinary exam and a fluorescein stain test to check for damage.

faqs.question: How can I safely restrain my cat to look at the eye?
faqs.answer: The 'burrito' method—wrapping your cat snugly in a towel—is often the safest way to keep them still while you inspect the eye or take a photo, preventing them from scratching you or further injuring themselves.
